coredata.dc:title: The role of fluorescent carbon dots in the fate of plastic waste
coredata.dc:creator: Hallaji, Zahra
Bagheri, Zeinab
Ranjbar, Bijan
coredata.dc:description: Today, due to the excessive use of plastic products, the pollution caused by them is increasing, and an efficient management system is needed to reduce their environmental effects. It is noteworthy that the risk of human ingestion can increase when plastic waste degrades into microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs). Additionally, as plastic ages and decomposes, common additives will continue to be released into the environment, having an incalculable and long-lasting harmful effect on the environment. As a result, several treatment strategies, such as recycling or upcycling, and degrading, have been evolved for the cleanup of this waste. Additionally, techniques for identifying and removing MPs, NPs, and plasticizers have been created. Researchers working in this area have recently become interested in carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterial, due to their simple and inexpensive synthesis from a variety of sources, fluorescence properties, and advantageous characteristics such as excellent biocompatibility, distinctive physicochemical properties, an abundance of functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl), high stability, and charge transport. Therefore, this article reviews the role of innovative CDs in the fate of plastics (waste, MPs, and plasticizers), including upcycling, sorting, detection, and photocatalytic degradation. Finally, a brief description of the issues and potential directions for future research in this area is provided.

head.ce:title: The role of fluorescent carbon dots in the fate of plastic waste

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Today, due to the excessive use of plastic products, the pollution caused by them is increasing, and an efficient management system is needed to reduce their environmental effects. It is noteworthy that the risk of human ingestion can increase when plastic waste degrades into microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs). Additionally, as plastic ages and decomposes, common additives will continue to be released into the environment, having an incalculable and long-lasting harmful effect on the environment. As a result, several treatment strategies, such as recycling or upcycling, and degrading, have been evolved for the cleanup of this waste. Additionally, techniques for identifying and removing MPs, NPs, and plasticizers have been created. Researchers working in this area have recently become interested in carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterial, due to their simple and inexpensive synthesis from a variety of sources, fluorescence properties, and advantageous characteristics such as excellent biocompatibility, distinctive physicochemical properties, an abundance of functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl), high stability, and charge transport. Therefore, this article reviews the role of innovative CDs in the fate of plastics (waste, MPs, and plasticizers), including upcycling, sorting, detection, and photocatalytic degradation. Finally, a brief description of the issues and potential directions for future research in this area is provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics are organic polymers that are semi-synthetic or synthetic and are frequently employed in modern life [1,2]. Unfortunately, the inefficient management of the fast-expanding plastics industry has resulted in enormous amounts of plastic waste in a variety of environmental areas [3–5]. Because of their resistance to corrosion, the majority of them are problematic to degrade and can remain in the environment for a century [6,7]. The effects of plastic waste on the environment are observable in the rising amounts of plastic pollution in the world's oceans and on land, which suggests that the world is subjected to the pressure of plastic-related environmental issues [8]. For instance, MPs constitute nearly 92% of the world's marine plastic debris. Therefore, they are widely spread in the marine environment [9]. Additionally, a variety of monomers and other materials, such as plasticizers, which increase a polymer's plasticity, are used to create plastics [10]. However, several of them, including phthalates (PAEs) and bisphenol A (BPA), have been found to be hazardous or endocrine disruptors. In the meantime, they are simply dislodged from the plastics over time due to the weak connections with the polymer components, posing significant environmental hazards [11,12].

A variety of strategies are considered essential to address the issue, including reducing and reusing plastics, better waste management, design for recycling and upcycling, detecting and tracking MPs and plasticizers, and in certain cases planning for open environment degradation [13,14]. Due to this, there has been a rising interest in upcycling plastic waste into products with value-added, such as fuels, carbon-based materials, and fine chemicals [15]. However, effectiveness in sorting plastic and constituent identification appears to be a technological challenge. The accuracy and speed of sorting plastic waste must be improved in order to boost the rate of recycling and upcycling [14]. Additionally, green and efficient photocatalysts may offer a path to plastics and bioplastics that are vulnerable to open environment degradation or moderate chemical recyclability [16].

Nowadays, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted considerable attention in different areas [17–24]. With intrinsic fluorescence capabilities and size below 10 nm, CDs are a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial that has evolved in recent years. These nanomaterials provide outstanding benefits such as ease of synthesis, suitable biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, and great photostability [25]. The impact of CDs on the fate of plastic waste is the subject of fascinating research and development. Based on our studies, in this field, the review article has not been published yet. Only a few articles have reviewed the synthesis and application of carbon nanomaterials from plastic waste. These articles have either only investigated the upcycling of plastic waste into different carbon nanomaterials [15,26–28], or they have only investigated carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNT) [29–31] or graphene [32]. However, this article's topic is dedicated to upcycling plastic waste into CDs and their other roles in the fate of plastic waste. First, solutions for removing plastic waste that is enabled by nanotechnology are simply described. Then, the definition of CDs, important features, and their synthesis methods were described in a concise and useful way. In the following, the roles and effects of CDs on the fate of plastic waste such as upcycling and sorting of plastic waste, detection and photocatalytic degradation of plastic waste, MPs and plasticizers were explained. Finally, the issues and potential directions for future researches in this area are also mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nano-enabled methods for removing plastic waste
ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of the environment with plastic waste is considered to be a severe threat to the ecology. Numerous technological advancements have been accomplished over the past five years, and ongoing research into new materials and methods is advancing the state of this field's development. One major enabling technology that has the ability to address a variety of social concerns is nanotechnology [33]. It has played important roles in a number of technological fields. Pollutant removal methods offered by nanotechnology have become effective ways to address problems with the status quo, either by improving performance or filling unfilled niche scopes [34–37]. A material's dimension has a significant impact on its efficiency in the applications, which they are intended for it. The structure of bulk materials has been substantially transformed by nanomaterials created by top-down or bottom-up methods, which has significantly impacted their properties [38]. Nano-enabled methods have compelling prospects for a notable improvement in the detection and monitoring, categorization and identification, as well as the removal efficiency in the field of plastic waste treatment and management.

A type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials known as CDs is those that have lateral diameters under 10 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.34 and 0.21 nm that correspond to the (002) and (100) lattice planes of graphite, respectively [39,40]. Suitable precursors are selected with the support of specific solvents and catalysts to generate CDs with particular structures and optical properties since the discovery of CDs in 2004 [41,42]. In addition, a variety of precursors, such as small organic molecules [43–45], polymers [46], carbon fibers [47], and natural resources [48,49], are frequently used in the production of CDs, which have drawn a lot of interest because of their abundance of sources, ease of availability, and distinctive optical properties. On their surface, CDs typically have different functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy to increase their hydrophilicity, and special chemical characteristics [50,51]. Comparing CDs to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, CDs show improved photostability, biocompatibility, and decreased toxicity [39,52]. In addition to having unusually effective multiphoton up-conversion and highly controllable photoluminescence (PL) from the deep ultraviolet (UV) to the near-infrared (NIR), CDs also exhibit unique optical properties due to the edge, surface, and quantum confinement effect that can be controlled by heteroatom doping, size, shape, surface and edge functionalization [53–55]. Moreover, CDs are used as photocatalysts for chemical reactions, pollutant decomposition, water splitting, and CO2 reduction in the solar energy conversion process. This is possible due to the wide spectrum of light absorption, from UV to NIR, low-cost, excellent photostability, and electron transport [52]. In Fig. 1, we show the relationship between the mentioned characteristics of CDs and their role in the fate of plastic waste.

The majority of CDs come into one of three main categories of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). GQDs are anisotropic and often have longitudinal dimensions that are noticeably smaller than lateral dimensions. They are composed of one or more layers of graphene, with the edges of the sheets joined by chemical groups. CQDs clearly display a crystalline lattice. The subclass of CNDs comprises carbon nanomaterials without any discernible crystalline lattice, and the PL center is positioned differently for each form of CND. CNDs are almost always spherical or quasi-spherical (
Fig. 2 ) [56]. In this article, for the uniformity of the text and to prevent the reader from confusing, the general term CDs is used to name these nanomaterials.

Top-down and bottom-up approaches can be used to synthesize CDs (
Fig. 3 ). In the top-down strategy, carbonaceous materials are cut or broken-down using chemical, electrochemical, or physical methods. The bottom-up strategy is accomplished by heating small organic molecules or by chemically fusing small aromatic molecules in multi-steps [59]. Due to the exact control of precursor molecules, ease of procedures, low-cost, feasibility, and simplicity of the operation with generally harmless precursors, the growth of bottom-up techniques for the manufacturing of CDs has recently attracted a lot of interest [60]. As can be seen in Table 1, CDs that are effective in the fate of plastic waste are mostly synthesized by bottom-up methods. Therefore, in this section, the focus will be on the bottom-up approaches.

The preferred process for the synthesis of CDs has been identified as thermal decomposition, which is carried out at high temperatures through the pyrolysis or thermal calcination of the carbon precursors. The pyrolysis and thermal calcination methods involve heating a precursor in the absence of oxygen and in the presence of inert gas, respectively. In the pyrolysis method, because it is difficult to create an oxygen-free environment, some oxygen may be present. The aforementioned processes have the benefits of wider precursor tolerance, solvent-free methods, and simplified operations. Additionally, by making changes to important variables like temperature and time, the optical properties of CDs are tuned [61,62]. In addition, the most widely used and popular methodology for the synthesis of simple, doped, and supported CDs is undoubtedly the hydro/solvothermal process. Precursor and solvent are mixed in the suitable proportions, added to the autoclave, which is sealed, and heated in an oven under the specified conditions for the reaction. Heating a precursor solution in water is a special characteristic of the hydrothermal process. This method's importance stems from the fact that most precursors dissolve in a solvent when temperature and pressure are changed. The benefits of this approach are the one-step synthetic process, relatively mild working conditions, and good solution dispersion [63,64]. Moreover, studies have demonstrated that microwaves are electromagnetic waves with wavelengths between 1 mm and 1 m, which have the potential to deliver high energy for breaking chemical bonds in a substrate. It has been thought that using microwave technology is an efficient way to speed up reactions and provide simultaneous homogeneous heating that contributes to the CDs' smooth size distribution [62,65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of CDs in the fate of plastic waste

ce:section.ce:para: Diverse recycling solutions have been created to address the issues with plastic, but in reality, they confront significant economic and technological difficulties. Upcycling, which attempts to obtain value-added products from plastic waste, is an interesting substitute [73]. Upcycling is actually a type of recycling where the manufactured item has a higher value than the plastic waste used to make it. Thus, researchers should attempt to eliminate the plastic problem by upcycling plastic waste into valuable advanced materials.

The content of carbon for PE, PP, and PS is 86, 86, and 92 wt%, respectively [28]. Heteroatoms added as a substitute for carbon atoms or surface functional groups, in addition to carbon and hydrogen atoms, play a discernible effect on CD performance. At this point, the carbon sources utilized to create CDs from plastic waste can also act as precursors to heteroatoms. PET and PLA have a carbon content of 62.5 and 50.4 wt%, respectively, based on their molecular structures, and they also contain extra oxygen heteroatoms [28,74]. Although the carbon network's strong electronegativity and size make direct substitutional oxygen doping very challenging [75], residual oxygen-containing groups within the structure of CDs have favorable effects that support specific CD characteristics, such as electrochemical features [76]. To create active sites that include nitrogen, PU can operate as a carbon and nitrogen source. PU includes 65 and 9 wt% of carbon and nitrogen atoms, respectively. To meet the physicochemical requirements of various commodities, commercial PU is typically made up of hard and soft segments (hard: diisocyanates and chain extenders, soft: polyester, polyether, and oligomeric polyols) [77]. As a result, different PU precursors have different carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen contents, which has an impact on the properties of related CDs. More critically, the PU treatment procedure would produce hazardous CO and HCN [78]. Thus, when PU is used as a precursor for CDs, a particular concern has been raised.

As a result, the abundance of carbon chains found in plastic waste has a significant potential to serve as a new source of carbon for the production of CDs. In this sense, PS, PE, and PP have the highest carbon content among the plastic waste that has been used for CD synthesis. In terms of chemical structure and composition, PS has a large number of benzene rings and the highest carbon content, which provides the conditions for PS to be the superior precursor of CDs. In competition with PS, we have PET, which, despite its lower carbon content, can be a good precursor for the synthesis of CDs due to the presence of benzene rings and oxygen heteroatoms in its structure. PLA has a lower carbon content than others and since it is one of the biodegradable polymers, even though the degradability process is slow, it seems that it is not an ideal option for upcycling to CDs. The use of PU is also not suitable due to the production of toxic compounds mentioned earlier. In addition, as can be seen in Table 1, based on our studies, there are few reports about upcycling of PLA and PU to CDs [72,79].

Due to the abundance and diversity of plastic waste as well as the processing methods that have been developed, the conversion of plastic waste to CDs may expand in terms of uses and to a lesser extent, the advantage of carbon sequestration. However, due to the complexity of plastic waste, extensive research is still needed for CDs derived from plastic waste and their utilization in various applications. In the following, we will review some of the reports in this field, which are categorized based on the type of polymer waste used.

Polystyrene (PS). An inert and hydrocarbon thermoplastic is expanded PS [67]. Expanded PS used for packaging is thrown away in enormous quantities these days. Therefore, creating an efficient way to utilize waste products is desirable.

In a study, waste-expanded PS was used as the precursor to creating CDs utilizing a one-step solvothermal process. Their PL had an adjustable spectrum that ranged from white to yellow and orange (
Fig. 5 a). White and multicolored light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were made using CD powder [67]. Another study created a new and simple synthesis technique for making CDs from waste PS foam and regular organic solvents. The produced CDs can mark multiple cellular structures concurrently and have controllable fluorescence ranging from blue to orange [80]. For the large-scale production of nitrogen doped CDs, waste PS was used by another group. This may be a promising scaling-up method and CDs are used as fluorescent probes to show the selective and sensitive detection of Au3+ ions [81]. Additionally, a straightforward method for producing strong solid protonic acids from PS waste has been published. It involves stirring at 130 °C for 3 h, accompanied by heating at 220 °C or 260 °C, respectively, for 2 h. They were discovered to be effective and recyclable catalysts for the esterification reaction [82]. Moreover, it was suggested to convert PS polymers to CDs using a two-step process that included nitration and solvothermal processing. CDs showed sensitive fluorescence quenching to Hg2+ and Fe3+. While CDs/Hg2+ fluorescence was restored by the addition of GSH, there was no significant change in the sample of CDs/Fe3+. As a result, a sensing assay for Hg2+, Fe3+, and GSH was created, and the Hg2+ sensing performance was shown using river samples as the model [83].

Polyethylene (PE) and Polypropylene (PP). The largest segment of the city's solid waste plastics is made up of polyolefins (PE and PP) [84]. Therefore, it is important for researchers to attempt recycling this plastic waste.

Plastic bags and surgical masks are in particularly high demand due to the COVID-19 pandemic. PE-based plastic bags and PP-based surgical masks have been claimed to be converted into CDs using an easy and one-step hydrothermal process with a 96% production yield. The created CDs can be dissolved in water and various organic solvents. Using fluorescent and flexible sheets made of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) doped with CDs, they show how CDs might be used as anti-counterfeiting tools. This synthesis technique provides a low-cost, straightforward, scalable, and contamination-resistant way to reuse plastic waste [70]. Another group describes the creation of PL polymer CDs from plastic waste. PP plastic waste was upcycled utilizing a straightforward heating procedure at temperatures close to its melting point of 200 °C, 250 °C, and 300 °C. Because of the heating procedure utilized in the upcycling process, PP plastic waste structural changes result in altered polymer CD emission spectra ( Fig. 5 b). The major component of polymer CDs, the CO carbonyl group, is formed when oxygen from the environment is bound by the carbon chain when PP plastic waste is heated. Polymer CDs have vast potential as a photocatalyst, bioimaging agent, and sensor for optoelectronic products [85].

The most widely produced plastic, PE, is primarily utilized in packaging. Using waste PE, two important interrelated results were reported. First, they can be converted into CDs with photocatalytic properties by using H2O2 as a non-residual oxidizing agent in an acidic medium, and they have the ability to enrich the reaction medium with a large amount of molecular oxygen in the air. Therefore, there is no need for a separate source of oxygen and they provide easy photocatalytic oxidation reactions. Second, when CDs do not participate in any photocatalytic reaction, they are completely oxidized and converted into CO2 due to sunlight (CD-autophagy). From the industrial point of view, separating CDs from the solution is a difficult task due to their very high solubility. This problem is completely solved with this self-extinction method, and because the production of CDs does not cost much, from the economic point of view, it is better to throw them away with the assumption that they will be phased out from the environment [68]. In another study using PE waste, an interesting example of the chemical conversion of an alkane to PE-CDs has been performed, where acid dehydrogenation of a PE backbone leads to the formation of alkenes. Then they undergo a cyclic reaction and form benzene derivatives and finally small pieces of functionalized graphene. Additionally, cyclization with the incorrect carbon atom will result in defective graphene derivatives with increased oxygen affinity. Controlled oxidation can be used to remove the carbon atoms outside of the graphitic network, creating PE-CDs. The results indicate an increase in the dissolution of molecular oxygen in the water in the presence of PE-CDs. This phenomenon is very useful in photocatalytic oxidation reactions because it is possible to carry out the reaction in the air without the need for an oxygen-rich environment [69].

Polyurethane (PU). Flexible PU foam is frequently utilized in the furniture, automotive, and consumer product industries [86]. Large amounts of waste are produced as a result of the high demand for PU foam. To solve this problem, several waste management strategies such as landfilling, incineration, and recycling have been developed, but each of them has disadvantages. Due to having a nitrogen atom in its structure, PU is an excellent precursor for the synthesis of CDs because this atom achieves high PL. As a result, this is an effective waste management plan for PU, but a particular concern has been raised when PU is used as a precursor for CDs due to the production of hazardous CO and HCN. However, in a study CDs were created using a one-step pyrolysis process starting with PU foam. PU foam was pyrolyzed with H2SO4 (3 M) at 200 °C for 6 h and a QY of 33% was achieved. The CDs served as sensors for Ag+ detection in comparison to various metal ions (
Fig. 6 ) [79].

Polyethylene terephthalate (PET). Huge amounts of solid waste have been produced as a result of the rising use of PET, a polymer that is useful for packaging, containers, films, and fibers [87]. This PET waste should be appropriately disposed of as they pose an environmental risk and are not biodegradable. Typically, PET waste is recycled into polyester fibers, strapping, and non-food containers or incinerated to provide energy [88]. Alternative processing methods for PET waste have recently been suggested as prospective feedstocks for the low-cost production of carbon-based nanomaterials such as CNT and graphene [28,89,90]. However, the possibility of converting PET waste into CDs is also possible.

An environmentally friendly method for producing CDs from PET bottles was reported using air oxidation and hydrothermal treatment with H2O2 (
Fig. 7 a). The resulting CDs demonstrated ferric ion (Fe3+) detection via a fluorescence quenching effect (on-off). Pyrophosphate anion (PPi) was able to precisely restore the fluorescence of CDs that had been particularly quenched by Fe3+, making the CDs/Fe3+ sensor feasible for PPi detection. An electron transfer mechanism was given to explain the sensing mechanism [88]. In another study, PET was converted into CDs in the presence and in the absence of an oxidizing agent, using the direct hydrothermal method and hydrothermal method that before synthesis, PET was affected by pyrolysis, glycolysis or aminolysis, until finally comparing their fluorescence properties. The direct hydrothermal method and methods with pyrolysis and glycolysis lead to the absence of fluorescence or weak fluorescence, while the method with aminolysis increases the fluorescence of the final product by about a hundred times. Moreover, the fluorescence of CDs produced from a hydrothermal mixture was further increased by adding a few percent of the oxidant H2O2 ( Fig. 7 b-e) [91]. In addition, PET bottle waste was converted into a CD using air oxidation in the presence of H2SO4. These nanoparticles were used as a new solid acid to convert fructose to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) due to their nano size and many acidic groups [92]. In recent work, CDs created from PET waste were used to treat pea (Pisum sativum) seeds as nano-priming agents ( Fig. 7 f). A promising technique used in agronomy to encourage seed germination and plant growth is seed nano-priming. In comparison to the control groups, the results showed that seed priming had positive effects at all CD concentrations (0.25–2 mg/mL), including quicker seed germination rate, increased shoot and root elongation, biomass accumulation, and root moisture level. After CD priming, seed coat surface degradation was seen, which significantly improved seed imbibition ability. Additionally, the CD-plant interaction markedly increased the activity of antioxidant enzymes in seeds and increased root vigor, chlorophyll, and carbohydrate content. These findings show that waste-derived CDs have considerable potential as nano-priming agents for seed germination by a low-cost and sustainable method [93].

Polylactic acid (PLA). PLA replaces petroleum-based plastics in many commercial applications such as flexible packaging, disposable cups, or shopping bags [72]. In spite of its well-known biodegradability, its degradation in marine environments is slow, causing serious environmental issues. Thanks to the presence of hydrolyzable ester bonds, this polymer is easily divided into its constituent oligomers through hydrolytic decomposition reactions [94]. These oligomers can be used as a carbon-containing platform for the synthesis of CDs. In a study, water-soluble PLA oligomers that had obtained after 3 weeks of hydrolytic degradation of PLA, were used to create spherical CDs in a one-step hydrothermal reaction. According to the findings, CDs are made up of a carbon core with a sp2 domain with carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups, which causes them to disperse in the polar environment. The time and temperature of the reaction as well as the incorporation of nitrogen groups in the CD structure affect the optical characteristics of the produced CDs (
Fig. 8 ) [72].

To reduce the environmental impact of plastic waste and the consumption of fossil resources used in their production, recycling plastic waste is an essential practice. However, the efficiency of sorting plastics and identifying their components is still an important challenge in this process [95]. To increase the recycling rate of plastic waste, the accuracy and speed of sorting should be improved. One of the best effective methods in this regard is the addition of fluorescent markers (FMs), which allow good sorting. In recent years have seen a lot of interest in the application of FMs as a precise and economical method for identifying and sorting plastic waste polymers [96–98]. FMs can be incorporated into the polymer matrix via extrusion molding and injection or even applied as a thin fluorescent coating on the final plastic product or intermediate material [99]. However, there are several challenges when using fluorescent particles/dyes such as high-cost, toxicity, and weak photostability at relevant industrial scales [14]. Meanwhile, CDs are a suitable alternative to fluorescent dyes and inorganic QDs due to their low toxicity, low-cost, good solubility, and high photostability [100].

MPs. The issue of plastic debris has been the center of attention for environmental issues in recent years. MPs and NPs produced from all kinds of plastics are everywhere in the environment. Understanding how they form and in which direction they move greatly helps to study their impact on the environment and human health. Generally, MPs are plastic particles with a diameter of less than 5 mm [101–104]. In addition, nano-sized plastic particles are considered with a diameter between 1 and 100 or 1000 nm [105–107]. MPs and NPs were produced during the degradation process of plastic waste and accumulated over time [108,109]. The path of environmental degradation of plastic as well as the production of MPs and NPs are summarized in Fig. 10. In the continuation of this section, since the use of CDs for the detection of NPs has not been reported based on our studies, we will only explain their use in the detection of MPs.

Today, various methods such as Fourier transform infrared microscopy (FT-IR) [112,113], Raman [114–116], and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GCMS) [117–119] are utilized to recognize the type and amount of MPs in environmental samples [120]. However, low-throughput and long-time measurement are restrictions of these methods. In addition, in this field, the miniaturization of tools is still a challenge, and the application of complex tools in real-time environmental monitoring is limited. For several years, fluorescence sensors have attracted much attention due to their high sensitivity and selectivity. However, in the field of MP research, they face challenges such as background and false positive signals. Fluorescence analysis in “on” mode can reduce the background interferences and show the true state of the samples. Carbon nanomaterials are new candidates in this field due to their unique properties. For example, a study reported the affinity of CNT for MPs [121].

Plasticizers. BPA and PAE are among the most popular plasticizer that is added to the polymer to increase their plasticity [122–124]. In the following, we will investigate the role of CDs in the detection of these two important plasticizers.

BPA. BPA is mainly used in the production of PC plastics and epoxy resins [125]. When these plastics or resins are exposed to heat, acid, or base, the ester bonds between the BPA monomers are hydrolyzed and BPA is released into the environment [125,126]. BPA is an endocrine-disrupting chemical due to its estrogen-like properties [127]. In addition, BPA is an environmental pollutant due to its poor biodegradability [128]. Therefore, the detection of BPA is very important and it is necessary to develop new methods with simple operation, high sensitivity, excellent reproducibility, and good selectivity. CDs usually show good biocompatibility and are widely used as biosensors [129–132].

In a work, silver nitrate (AgNO3)-trisodium citrate (TSC) catalysis using CDs has been used to produce silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs). At a certain concentration of AgNO3-TSC, an effective collision between citrate and Ag+ rarely occurs. After the addition of CDs, citrate and Ag+ are absorbed on their surface, and electrons are transferred from citrate to Ag+ and leading to the production of Ag, 1,3-acetone dicarboxylic acid, and CO2. The amount of Ag-NPs increases with the increasing loading of CDs (
Fig. 12 a). In this system, the SERS signal is strong with Ag-NPs as the SERS substrate and Victoria blue B (VBB) as the molecular probe. In the presence of aptamer (Apt), the surface of CDs is covered and the absorption of citrate and Ag+ on the CD surface is prevented. As a result, its catalytic activity is inhibited and SERS intensity decreases. In the presence of BPA, a specific bond is formed with Apt, thereby making the surface of CDs available again and restoring catalytic activity. In the presence of BPA, the produced Ag-NPs increase, and the SERS signal becomes intense, so it is possible to develop a method for BPA detection [133]. A group of researchers has developed an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor for the specific detection of BPA. In this system, functionalized nitrogen doped CDs with polyethyleneimine (NCDs@PEI) and Ru
( bpy )
3
2 +
nanosheets (RuNSs) are a co-reactant and luminophore, respectively ( Fig. 12 b). Due to the presence of NCDs and PEI in RuNSs, the developed aptasensor showed better ECL characteristics. In order to further increase the selectivity of the system, Apt of BPA was also added to make a new ECL aptasensor, which showed better performance compared to the system without Apt. Finally, when the results of the ECL aptasensor for the analysis of BPA in water samples were compared with the high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) method, its appropriate accuracy was confirmed [134]. In another report, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for BPA was developed based on CDs. Due to the presence of a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the CD's surface; it is possible to coordinate the polymerization of Eu3+ and 5⸍-adenosine monophosphate on the CD surface to form a sensor. The results showed that the ratio of fluorescence intensity and BPA concentration have a good linear relationship. In addition, the fluorescence color change of the system with different concentrations of BPA can be easily observed under a UV lamp with the naked eye, which shows the potential of this detection method for quick and convenient monitoring of BPA in real water samples [135].

PAE. The addition of PAE can increase the mechanical properties of plastic materials [136]. However, it has been reported that their decomposition products cause serious damage to human health [137]. In addition, the widespread use of plastic products accelerates the transfer of PAEs from these products to the aquatic environment [138]. Common PAEs have more than 17 types [139,140]. Therefore, monitoring their concentration in the environment and food samples is important to protect human health. In this field, CDs have opened a new way of research.

In one study, red CDs (R-CDs) conjugated with an anti-diethyl phthalate (DEP) antibody were used to detect DEP. The antibody can recognize and bind to the immobilized antigen. Antibody binding quenches R-CD's fluorescence. In the next step, formamide is added, and the fluorescence of R-CD in this solution turns on due to the change of electron distribution on the surface and electronic charge transfer. If there was DEP in this environment, it competes with the antigen, and the conjugated R-CD is attached to it, which eventually causes the quench of R-CDs (
Fig. 13 a) [141]. To detect di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP), a photoelectrochemical (PEC) platform was constructed using arrays of CD-decorated TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2-NTs) as a transducer species and anti-DEHP Apt as a biological detection element. Combining CDs with TiO2-NTs resulted in increased visible light absorption, enhanced charge separation and transfer, excellent photocurrent response, and increased PEC activity. After that, the binding of Apt and BSA will decrease photocurrent density. Finally, with the connection of DEHP, this photocurrent density decreases again and leads to its identification ( Fig. 13 b) [142].

Plastic waste. One of the approaches to reducing the problem of plastic waste is environmental degradation [143]. Green and efficient photocatalysts can be a suitable route for sensitive plastics and bioplastics to degradation [144,145]. Photocatalytic materials are catalysts that convert organic materials into small molecules in the presence of light [146].

MPs. An effective method for reducing environmental pollution and the energy issue is the efficient conversion of MPs into fuels. Nevertheless, low product selectivity and possible secondary contamination present a barrier to conventional procedures.

By combining Methanosarcina barkeri (M. b) and CD-functionalized polymeric carbon nitrides (CD-PCN), a biotic-abiotic photocatalytic system is created that allows biodegradable MPs of PLA converted into CH4 after heat pretreatment for five consecutive 24-day cycles with ∼100% CH4 selectivity with the help of extra CO2. The simultaneous operation of photo oxidation and reduction methanogenesis was demonstrated by mechanistic study. This was accomplished by fully using photogenerated holes and electrons without the use of chemical sacrificial quenchers. A new method for engineering the plastic's reuse has been proven by additional research using M. b-CD-PCN for non-biodegradable MPs to CH4 conversion [150].

Plasticizers. As mentioned earlier, BPA is an endocrine disruptor and many tests have shown its toxicity in animals [151]. Therefore, apart from detecting this material, there is a need to find suitable methods to remove it.

A system including CDs and MIL-101(Fe) (CD/MIL-101(Fe)) was established for the activation of persulfate to decompose BPA under the effect of visible light. Upon visible light irradiation, both the MIL-101(Fe) and CD moieties are excited to generate charge carriers. The presence of CDs increases the absorption of visible light, which leads to the production of more electrons and holes. In addition, the large contact area and high surface area of the composite induce the transfer of photogenerated electrons from CD to MIL-101(Fe), which can facilitate the conversion of Fe3+ center to Fe2+ and persulfate activate to produce SO4 •- radicals that some of these radicals can oxidize OH- and produce •OH. The next point is that the electrons that are transferred to the CD surface cause the conversion of O2 attached to the material to •O2 - radicals. Free radicals lead to the rapid decomposition of BPA and meanwhile, valence band holes interact with BPA, this degradation mechanism is shown in Fig. 15 [151]. A carbon nitride polymer (PCN) was functionalized with CD and used as a photocatalyst to activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for BPA degradation under visible light irradiation. The incorporation of CDs into PCN resulted in increased surface area, enhanced light absorption, and better charge separation, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic activation of PMS. As a result, PCN functionalized with CD showed better performance in photocatalytic activation of PMS for BPA removal [152].

Precursors, the preparation method of plastic waste, the synthesis method of CDs, the emission color of CDs, QY, biocompatibility, the role of CDs in the fate of plastic waste, and the application of CDs reported in the literature were summarized in Table 1.

The rapid increase in the production of plastic waste leads to adverse effects on the environment, so the development of different strategies is needed to effectively manage this issue. This review provided a brief assessment of one of the possible options for addressing the plastic problem. We summarized recent advances in upcycling plastic waste to CDs. Then, we focused on the use of CDs for sorting, detection, and photocatalytic degradation of plastic waste, MPs, and plasticizers. Despite the interesting research that has been done in this field to date, several issues should be given more attention:
1)
The synthetic methods that have been used to convert plastic waste into CDs generally are long time and require high energy. There are faster synthesis methods such as microwave-assisted, which have been less discussed in this field ( Table 1 ), despite the production of high-quality CDs. Optimization of processes and widespread use of rapid conversion methods can accelerate the upcycling of plastic waste.
2)
Future study must focus on the entire conversion of plastic waste into CDs during synthesis, which requires the selection of the best synthesis process, plastic waste, and reaction parameters. This would increase the yield of these nanomaterials and reduce the loss of the precursor and the formation of hazardous compounds during synthesis. The byproducts of the breakdown process could be hazardous and difficult to collect. Therefore, active catalysts should be carefully made to minimize the creation of harmful compounds by maximizing carbonization efficiency, especially for the safe handling of PU.
3)
Moving lab-scale testing to actual implementation is the main goal of plastic upcycling. Contrary to pure polymers, real plastic waste may contain additives from the production process (such as dye, pigment, and metal) or adherent pollutants from the disposal process (such as oil, textile, and sand). In addition, the structural diversity and intricacy of real-world plastics complicate the CD synthesis process. Therefore, the convenience of carbonization depends on the preparation of plastic waste, which includes separation, sorting, cleaning, crushing, and grinding.
4)
Getting a high yield of CDs during the synthesizing process is still difficult. For the industrialization of various applications, it is necessary to produce high-performance CDs in a large scale using trustworthy and simple techniques with a conscious techno-economic assessment.
5)
Most CDs made of plastic waste have been used for sensing ( Table 1 ). Nevertheless, the use of these nanomaterials in other fields is also very important. The emergence of wider applications will solve the current challenge of plastic waste, as well as reduce the costs of manufacturing useful materials in various fields.
6)
Converting plastic waste into other valuable materials is a way to get rid of the pollution these materials cause in the environment. However, does turning these materials into nanomaterials like CDs really solve this issue, or does the environment face new problems? The dangers caused by nanomaterials in today's world are not yet fully known, and this issue should be considered in the actual application of nanomaterials obtained from plastic waste.
7)
CDs show low toxicity and good biocompatibility in low concentrations (up to 10 μg/mL). They also have the ability to leave the body through natural metabolic pathways and do not cause specific metabolic changes in the body [25]. However, studies on the biocompatibility of CDs should continue, especially when working on them in a new field. However, as can be seen in Table 1, very few reports have worked on determining the biocompatibility of their CDs from plastic waste. It is necessary to seriously examine this issue in future studies because it is one of the cases that can answer the previous challenge (the sixth case).
8)
Appropriate studies have been conducted in the field of detecting plasticizers using CDs, but more studies should be conducted in the case of MPs. In addition, studies on the use of CDs for sorting and photocatalytic degradation of plastic waste, MPs, and plasticizers are still in their early stages and it is necessary to pay more attention to these applications in the future and conduct more studies in this field.
9)
Finally, isn't turning plastic waste into CDs a way to produce MPs and NPs more easily and release plasticizers and additives? Given that, production methods are generally not gentle.
Not applicable.
